coredata.dc:title: Preparation of SnOx/N-GQDs composites with oxygen-rich vacancies for enhanced photoelectric properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yongqin
Feng, Tao
Li, Lin
Lei, Yun
Du, Beibei
Luo, Linhui
Li, Can
Deng, Yifan
Tang, Zehui
Chen, Jiong
coredata.dc:description: SnOx with oxygen-rich vacancies and SnOx/N-GQDs (Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots) composites were prepared by high temperature annealing and hydrothermal method, respectively. The annealing process introduced a large number of oxygen vacancies and produced defect energy levels in the band gap. The existence of the defect level effectively reduced the band gap of SnOx, enlarged the optical absorption range, and improved the light utilization. The introduction of N-GQDs also improved the carrier density and electron transport rate, and extended the carrier lifetime (τ = 7.44 ms). I-T (Transient photocurrent response) analysis results showed that when the mass ratio of Sn/SnO2 was 1:4 and N-GQDs doping content was 1 wt%, the transient photocurrent response of SnOx/N-GQDs could reach 5.51 × 10−4 A/cm2, which was 25 times higher than that of pure SnO2, and the photocurrent response value maintained 80.1 % within 500 s. EIS (Electrochemical impedance) and M-S (Mott-Schottky) analyses indicated that the appropriate amount of oxygen vacancies and N-GQDs were beneficial to reducing the charge transport resistance, inhibiting the electron-hole pair recombination, and increasing the carrier density of SnOx. The results show that SnOx/N-GQDs composites present the improved photoelectric properties with the addition of oxygen vacancies and N-GQDs, which provides a new idea for promoting the properties of semiconductors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Oxygen vacancy
Photoelectric properties
SnOx/N-GQDs
Nano synthesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of SnO2, SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs.
TEM (a) and HRTEM images (b) of SnOx/N-GQDs.
Raman spectra of SnO2, SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs.
XPS high-resolution spectra: Sn 3d (a) and O 1s (b) of SnO2; Sn 3d (c) and O 1s (d) of SnOx; Sn 3d (e) and O 1s (f) of SnOx/N-GQDs.
XPS high-resolution spectra of SnOx/N-GQDs: C 1s (a) and N 1s (b).
FTIR spectra of SnO2, N-GQDs, SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs.
The N2 adsorption- isotherm (a) and pore-size distribution curve (b) of SnOx/N-GQDs.
Transient photocurrent response curves of SnO2, SnOx (a) and SnOx/N-GQDs (b).
Linear sweep voltammetry curves of SnO2, SnOx (a) and SnOx/N-GQDs (b).
Electrochemical impedance plots of SnO2 and SnOx: dark field (a) and light field (b).
Electrochemical impedance plots of SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs (a), Bode plots of SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs (b).
Mott Schottky curves of SnO2, SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs.
Schematic diagram of the preparation process of SnOx/N-GQDs composites.
The percentage of oxygen species in the O 1 s spectra.

head.ce:title: Preparation of SnOx/N-GQDs composites with oxygen-rich vacancies for enhanced photoelectric properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guizhou University of Engineering Science, Bijie City 551700, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guizhou University of Engineering Science, Bijie City, 551700, Peoples R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, Peoples R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guoxin International Engineering Consulting Group Co., LTD, Hubei branch, Wuhan 430064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guoxin International Engineering Consulting Group Co., LTD

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guoxin International Engineering Consulting Group Co., LTD. Hubei branch, Wuhan, 430064, Peoples R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: SnOx with oxygen-rich vacancies and SnOx/N-GQDs (Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots) composites were prepared by high temperature annealing and hydrothermal method, respectively. The annealing process introduced a large number of oxygen vacancies and produced defect energy levels in the band gap. The existence of the defect level effectively reduced the band gap of SnOx, enlarged the optical absorption range, and improved the light utilization. The introduction of N-GQDs also improved the carrier density and electron transport rate, and extended the carrier lifetime (τ = 7.44 ms). I-T (Transient photocurrent response) analysis results showed that when the mass ratio of Sn/SnO2 was 1:4 and N-GQDs doping content was 1 wt%, the transient photocurrent response of SnOx/N-GQDs could reach 5.51 × 10−4 A/cm2, which was 25 times higher than that of pure SnO2, and the photocurrent response value maintained 80.1 % within 500 s. EIS (Electrochemical impedance) and M-S (Mott-Schottky) analyses indicated that the appropriate amount of oxygen vacancies and N-GQDs were beneficial to reducing the charge transport resistance, inhibiting the electron-hole pair recombination, and increasing the carrier density of SnOx. The results show that SnOx/N-GQDs composites present the improved photoelectric properties with the addition of oxygen vacancies and N-GQDs, which provides a new idea for promoting the properties of semiconductors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Additional data will be made available upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: Among all kinds of metal oxides, tin dioxide (SnO2), as a typical n-type semiconductor [1], is widely used in gas sensors [2,3], lithium/sodium batteries [4,5], supercapacitors [6,7], solar cells [8,9], etc. However, due to the wide bandgap [10] and low charge separation efficiency [11], the photoelectric properties of SnO2 are limited. Therefore, narrowing the band gap of SnO2 and increasing the charge separation efficiency are the keys to improving the photoelectric properties of SnO2.

In order to enhance the photoelectric properties of SnO2, two strategies are adopted as follows. Reducing the electron-hole pair recombination rate is the one strategy that can effectively improve the photoelectric performance of semiconductors [12]. The construction of heterojunctions is one of the effective strategies to diminish the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs by changing the electron transfer path. The photoelectric properties of SnO2 can be improved by selecting appropriate materials to combine and form composite structures. In recent years, graphene with excellent electronic structure and special photoelectric properties [13] has attracted wide attention. Graphene is a two-dimensional [14] flat material composed of carbon atoms in sp2 hybrid orbitals, which was prepared by chemical reduction of graphite oxide (GO) using graphite as raw material. Due to the special properties of graphene, it has a wide range of applications in spintronics, micro and nano processing, sensors and other fields. N-GQDs have high carrier mobility and can easily be combined with SnO2 through electrostatic interaction [15–17], making it an excellent material for loading SnO2 semiconductors. The other important strategy is to change the crystal structure of SnO2 by introducing oxygen vacancy defects, so as to optimize its band gap and improve its photoelectric properties. The present studies have shown that many metal oxide semiconductors (MOS) have oxygen vacancies which can change the electronic structure and chemical properties of materials, and can be widely used in sensing [18,19], catalysis [20,21], battery [22,23], supercapacitors [24,25] and other fields [26,27]. As a basic point defect, oxygen vacancy can introduce a donor energy level below the MOS conductor band to reduce its band gap width and promote the absorption of visible light, thus extending the edge of its light absorption band to visible or even near-infrared bands [28]. In addition, oxygen vacancies can serve as shallow donors, providing electrons and ensuring their continuous transport. It can also play the role of an electron trap, improve the lifetime of electrons, promote the carrier mobility, and provide more active sites [29,30]. Zeng et al. [31] successfully prepared SnO2 samples with different oxygen vacancy concentrations by NaBH4 treatment, which had better ethanol sensing properties. Huang et al. [32] prepared SnO2-x/g-C3N4 photocatalyst by partial oxidation of Sn2+, which could effectively remove antibiotics and inactivate bacteria. Wu et al. [19] revealed that the gas-sensitive properties of SnO2 nanotubes were dependent on oxygen vacancies generated by Al doping. The defect energy level introduced by oxygen vacancy can effectively reduce the band gap, and meanwhile N-GQDs can accelerate electron transport and effectively suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs. The combination of oxygen vacancy and N-GQDs can effectively enhance the photoelectric properties of the composites. Studies have shown that oxygen vacancies or carbon-based materials have good effects on improving the performance of hydrogen evolution [33], methanol photothermal degradation [34], supercapacitors [35] and lithium-ion batteries [36], but there are few studies on the performance improvement of SnO2 by oxygen vacancies and N-GQDs, especially in terms of photoelectric performance.

In this work, the composites with abundant oxygen vacancies were prepared by thermal annealing and simple hydrothermal method, and the effects of N-GQDs and oxygen vacancy on the photoelectric properties of SnO2 were investigated. The oxygen vacancy reduced the band gap and promoted the carrier density, and N-GQDs accelerated the migration of carriers and the separation of electron-hole pairs.

Tin chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O), tin powder, urea (CO(NH2)2), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), hydrochloric acid (HCl 0.1 mol/L), anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH), ammonia (NH3·H2O, 28 wt%) and flake graphite (99.9 %). All raw materials are from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD.

GO was prepared by inserting oxygen-containing functional groups into graphite sheets by Hummers method through three stages of low temperature, medium temperature and high temperature [37]. Typically, 60 mg of GO were homogeneously dispersed in deionized water. Subsequently, 8 mL H2O2 and 2.4 mL NH3·H2O (28 wt%) were added, and the mixed solution was autoclaved in a Teflon-lined stainless steel vessel at 180 °C for 6 h. The products were centrifuged and collected, and the supernatant was filtered through a needle filter (0.22 μm) to obtain N-GQDs (1 mg/mL) [38,39].

SnO2 nanoparticles were obtained by the previous preparation method [15]. The tin powder was mixed with the prepared SnO2 nanoparticles by grinding and annealed at 500 °C in nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h. By controlling the weight ratio of tin powder to tin dioxide (1:3 1:4 1:5 1:6), different products were obtained and marked with SnOx-1, SnOx-2, SnOx-3, SnOx-4, respectively.

The preparation process of SnOx/N-GQDs composites was shown in Scheme 1 . 400 mg of SnOx (1:4) nanoparticles were mixed with different contents of N-GQDs (0.5 wt% 1.0 wt% 1.5 wt% 2.0 wt%) under continuous magnetic stirring for 4 h. Then, the mixed solution was reacted at 200 °C for 4 h. The reaction product was dried to obtain SnOx/N-GQDs nanoparticles and noted as SnOx/N-GQDs-0.5, SnOx/N-GQDs-1, SnOx/N-GQDs-1.5, SnOx/N-GQDs-2, respectively.

The composition and morphology of the composites were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/MAX-RB, Rigaku Corporation, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, FEI Corporation, USA). In XRD test, CuKα was used as the diffraction target (incident wavelength λ = 0.154 nm) with rated power of 3 kW. TEM imaging was conducted at a magnification of 106 and a voltage of 20 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on the Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument with an excitation source of Al Kα rays and an operating voltage of 12 kV. Raman spectroscopy is tested by HORIBA's LabRAM Odyssey instrument. Transient photocurrent response (I-T), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), electrochemical impedance (EIS), and Mott-Schottky (M-S) data were collected by an electrochemical workstation (CHI650E) with a three-electrode system, in which the sample coated FTO glass, platinum foil and Ag/AgCl acted as a working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively.

The phase composition of SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs composites was analyzed using XRD patterns, as shown in Fig. 1 . SnO2 exhibited four strong diffraction peaks at 2θ of 26.58°, 33.82°, 37.82°, and 51.74°. These peaks corresponded to the (110), (101), (200), and (211) crystal planes of the rutile structure SnO2 (JCPDS No.41-1445) [40]. The patterns of SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs showed several diffraction peaks different from those of SnO2, corresponding to the characteristic diffraction peaks of Sn (JCPDS No. 04-0673) [41] and SnO (JCPDS No.07-0195). No correlation peak of N-GQDs was detected in the SnOx/N-GQDs spectrum, which was mainly attributed to the low content of incorporated N-GQDs.

TEM images of SnOx/N-GQDs composites at different resolutions were shown in Fig. 2 . It was observed that the particles in the images are microsphere-shaped and uniformly distributed, and the diameter of these microspheres was about 5–10 nm (Fig. 2(a)). It could be seen from Fig. 2(b) that the 0.30 nm and 0.28 nm crystal plane spacings corresponded to the crystal face of SnO [42] and the (101) crystal face of Sn [22]. The distinct d-spacings of 0.24 nm and 0.34 nm corresponded to the (1120) and (002) crystal planes of N-GQDs [43–45]. Those of 0.26 nm and 0.33 nm corresponded to the (101) and (110) crystal planes of SnO2, which was in accordance with the XRD patterns of SnO2.

The defects of the different samples were analyzed with the help of Raman spectra (Fig. 3 ). The peaks of SnO2 at 352, 435, 571, 630, and 774 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration modes of Eu, Eg, As, A1g and B2g [46,47] with SnO2. Eg vibration mode represented the vibration parallel to the C-axis in the OO plane; A1g and B2g vibration mode represented the stretching vibration of Sn-O; As vibration mode was related to defects in materials [46]. It was observed that the peak of SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs samples at 571 cm−1 were significantly wider than that of SnO2, indicating an obvious defect in SnOx, which could be explained by the existence of a large number of oxygen vacancies in SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs [48]. In addition, two weak peaks could be observed at 1380 and 1591 cm−1 of SnOx/N-GQDs Raman spectra, belonging to the D band and G band of N-GQDs [49]. The intensity ratio of peaks D and G (ID/IG) was 0.86, indicating that N-GQDs had some defects with the doping of nitrogen, which was conducive to the improvement of photoelectric properties.

Fig. S1(a, b) showed the full XPS spectra of SnO2 and SnOx composites, in which Sn, O and C elements were detected. The high-resolution spectra Sn 3d and O 1s of SnO2 are shown in Fig. 4 (a, b). Sn 3d could be divided into a pair of peaks (487.00 eV and 495.42 eV) corresponding to Sn4+. O 1s could be divided into three peaks corresponding to lattice oxygen (OL) at the binding energy of 530.88 eV. 531.74 eV and 532.53 eV corresponded to vacancy oxygen (OV) and adsorbed oxygen (OC) [40]. Fig. 4(c, d) showed the Sn 3d and O1s of SnOx. Sn 3d could be divided into three pairs of peaks [50]. The binding energy corresponded to Sn4+ at 487.20 eV and 495.57 eV, Sn2+ at 486.56 eV and 495.00 eV, and Sn0 at 485.65 eV and 494.09 eV. This was caused by the formation of SnOx in the process of high-temperature annealing, which was consistent with the XRD analysis results. O 1s was fitted into three peaks with binding energies at 530.70 eV, 531.49 eV and 531.49 eV, corresponding to OL, OV and OC. According to the O 1 s fitting results of SnO2 and SnOx, the oxygen content was analyzed, as shown in the Table 1 . The vacancy oxygen content of SnOx (34.27 %) was significantly higher than that of SnO2 (18.35 %), which further indicated that a large number of oxygen vacancies were introduced in SnOx.

Fig. 4(e, f) showed the Sn 3d and O1s of SnOx/N-GQDs. The Sn 3d spectra could be fitted into three pairs of peaks for Sn4+, Sn2+ and Sn0, with binding energies of 495.43 eV and 487.09 eV for Sn4+, 494.96 eV and 486.57 eV for Sn2+ and 494.08 eV and 485.62 eV for Sn0, which was consistent with the SnOx with the Sn 3d peak splitting results. The O1s spectrum of SnOx/N-GQDs was fitted into three peaks corresponding to OL (530.62 eV), OV (531.63 eV) and OC (533.08 eV), which was calculated to be 34.64 % OV in the SnOx/N-GQDs composites (Table 1). The oxygen vacancy content of SnOx/N-GQDs was comparable to that of SnOx, which indicated that the addition of N-GQDs did not affect the oxygen vacancy of SnOx, which was in agreement with Raman's analysis.

The SnOx/N-GQDs was examined by XPS for further analysis of its elemental composition, and the results were shown in Fig. 5 . The peak for N 1s was observed in the full spectrum (Fig. S1(c)). The C 1s spectrum of SnOx/N-GQDs were fitted to three peaks (Fig. 5(a)), corresponding to the CC bond (284.80 eV), C-O-C (286.45 eV) and O-C=O bonds (288.80 eV), respectively [51]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 5(b)) was divided into four peaks centered at 399.08 eV (pyridine N), 399.99 eV (pyrrole N), 400.95 eV (graphite N) and 402.55 eV (oxidized N) and these peaks were derived from the introduction of N-GQDs [52].

To understand the functional group structure information of SnOx/N-GQDs, FTIR was used to analyze SnOx/N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 6 . The transmittance spectra centered at 1631 and 3434 cm−1 were assigned to the bending and stretching vibrations of OH [53,54]. The peaks at 536 and 660 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of SnO and Sn-O-Sn respectively, which belonged to the characteristic absorption peak of SnO2 [55]. 1132 cm−1 was C-OH bending vibration in N-GQDs. For N-GQDs and SnOx/N-GQDs samples, the peaks at 1384, 1400 and 3136 cm−1 corresponded to CN stretching vibration, OH stretching vibration in carboxyl group [56] and NH stretching vibration [57], respectively.

The specific surface area and pore-size distribution of SnOx/N-GQDs were presented in Fig. 7(a). The N2 adsorption isotherm of SnOx/N-GQDs were recognized as type IV isotherms with an obvious capillary condensation stage and an adsorption hysteresis ring in the middle section, suggesting the presence of abundant porous structure in the composites. The specific surface area of SnOx/N-GQDs is 61.12 m2/g. As shown in Figure7 (b), the pore size distribution range is 2-20 nm, belonging to good mesoporous materials. Among them, the total pore volume of SnOx/N-GQDs is large, and the average pore size is about 4.8 nm. BET and pore size distribution tests indicate that SnOx/N-GQDs have large specific surface area and abundant pore size, and there may be a large number of defects and active sites.

SnO2, SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs composites were illuminated using a 365 nm point light source, and I-T responses were collected by on/off cycles of intermittent ultraviolet-light irradiation (30s) at a bias potential of 0.4 V. Fig. 8 (a) showed the transient photocurrent response of SnO2 and SnOx. Compared with SnO2, the photoelectric response properties of SnOx were significantly improved, suggesting that SnOx had better light utilization and photoelectric conversion ability. Since the formation of oxygen vacancy led to lattice distortion and defects in SnO2, the band gap was narrowed and the light absorption was widened (Fig. S2). The defect energy level introduced by oxygen vacancy could be used as the trapping energy level to capture photogenerated electrons. Due to its location near the CB of SnO2, more electrons were easily transferred to the CB, which prevented the combination of photogenerated electrons and holes to a certain extent, increased the electron lifetime and improved the photoelectric properties of SnOx [2]. The results showed that the photocurrent density firstly increased and then decreased as the proportion of tin powder increased. The decreasing tendency was attributed to the increasing proportion of tin powder led to excessive oxygen vacancy in SnOx, resulting in a deep defect state. The excessive oxygen vacancy acted as a deep electron trap in SnOx. After trapping the photogenerated electrons, a strong binding is formed for them, hindering the further transfer of electrons, accelerating the attenuation of photoelectrons, reducing the number and lifetime of charge carriers, thus weakening the photocurrent. When the mass ratio of Sn to SnO2 was 1:4, the photocurrent response reached the maximum of 2.01 × 10−4 A/cm2, and the photocurrent of SnOx-2 was 9.48 times higher than that of SnO2 (2.12 × 10−5 A/cm2).

The influence of N-GQDs on the photocurrent response properties of SnOx-2 composites was shown in Fig. 8(b). Based on the advantage of oxygen vacancy, SnOx-2 showed good photoelectric response properties, and the addition of N-GQDs further enhanced the photoelectric properties of SnOx-2 composites. The formation of heterojunction and oxygen vacancy between N-GQDs and SnOx improved the transport mode of electrons and promoted the generation of more carriers in SnOx/N-GQDs. Under the combined action of oxygen vacancy and N-GQDs, the photogenerated electrons could be captured in time for further transmission, effectively avoiding the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Therefore, SnOx/N-GQDs further improved the photoelectric properties of SnOx-2, showing excellent photoelectric response properties. The content of N-GQDs also had a certain impact on the improvement of material properties. When the content was too large, the photocurrent density was found to decline. This was because excessive N-GQDs tended to agglomerate due to the high surface and small size, which affected the light absorption, storage and electron transmission, resulting in the decrease of photocurrent. When the doping amount of N-GQDs was 1 wt%, the photocurrent density of SnOx/N-GQDs-1 reached the maximum of 5.51 × 10−4 A/cm2, which was 2.74 times as high as that of SnOx (2.01 × 10−4 A/cm2), and 25 times higher than that of SnO2 (2.12 × 10−5 A/cm2). The result indicated that the combination of appropriate oxygen vacancy and N-GQDs could improve the photoelectric performance of SnO2. Fig. S3 showed the stability test of SnOx/N-GQDs-1. The photocurrent density of SnOx/N-GQDs-1 in the first cycle was 6.82 × 10−4 A/cm2, and that of the eighth cycle was 5.51 × 10−4 A/cm2. It could be seen that SnOx/N-GQDs-1 had good stability, and the transient photocurrent response value could be maintained at 80.8 % within 500 s.

Fig. 9 (a, b) showed the LSV test results of SnO2, SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs composites. The test results were consistent with the transient current response results. The current density reached the maximum at SnOx-2, which was significantly higher than that of SnO2 (Fig. 9(a)). The result showed that the introduction of an appropriate amount of oxygen vacancies could have an obvious improvement in the photoelectric properties of SnO2. N-GQDs had a positive effect on the improvement of the photocurrent density of SnOx-2 (Fig. 9(b). With the increase of the N-GQDs content, the photocurrent density firstly increased and then decreased, and the maximum photocurrent density was obtained at SnOx/N-GQDs-1, which had an obvious enhancement compared to SnOx-2.

The electrochemical impedance of the material played a key role in the photoelectric properties. Fig. 10 showed the EIS Nyquist diagram of SnO2 and SnOx under dark or light field. In the dark field (Fig. 10(a)), SnOx showed a smaller semi-arc radius in the high-frequency region, indicating that SnOx had a smaller interface charge-transfer impedance [58]. This was mainly attributed to the regulation effect of oxygen vacancy on the band gap of SnO2. Electrons were not directly excited from the VB to the CB, but first captured by the defect level below the CB and then transferred to the CB, which improved the interface charge transfer in SnOx. This transmission mode required less energy and reduced interface transfer resistance during the transmission process. The charge-transfer impedance of SnOx was further reduced under light field (Fig. 10(b)). This was because the UV point light source could provide the higher energy, promote the electron transfer, and present a smaller impedance.

The electrochemical impedance results of the SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs composites were shown in Fig. 11 (a), and the inset was the simulated equivalent circuit diagram for this system. CPE, Rw and Rct represented the double-layer capacitance, warburg impedance and charge transfer impedance, respectively. SnOx/N-GQDs showed a smaller impedance [22], which also indicated that N-GQDs facilitated the reduction of material impedance and promoted charge transfer. The optimum doping content of N-GQDs was 1 wt%, where the charge transfer resistance reached the minimum. Fig. 11(b) showed the Bode plots of SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs for the analysis of the carrier lifetime (τe). According to Eq:
(1)
τ e
=
1
2 π
f max

The calculated values of τe for SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs were 5.03 ms and 7.44 ms, respectively, and the carrier lifetime of SnOx/N-GQDs was longer than that of SnOx, which indicated that the formation of heterojunctions between N-GQDs and SnOx changed the electron transport pathway of SnOx, inhibited the electron-hole pair recombination, and prolonged the lifetime of photogenerated electrons [59].

The MS curves of SnO2, SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs under bright and dark fields were shown in Fig. 12 . According to the Mott-Schottky equation:
(2)
1
C SC 2
=
2
ε
ε 0
e
N D
E −
E FB
−
K B
T
q
(3)
N D
=
2
ε
ε 0
e
d
1
C SC 2
d
E
− 1

The slope of the curve can be used to analyze the carrier density (ND) of the material. The slope of this region was inversely proportional to ND of the material. Compared with SnO2, the slope of SnOx was significantly reduced, indicating that SnOx had a higher carrier density. Because oxygen vacancy could act as the donor layer below the SnO2 CB, capturing and transferring more photoelectrons to the CB quickly, thus generating an enhanced carrier density. Compared with SnOx, SnOx/N-GQDs presented a higher carrier density. This was explained by the fact that N-GQDs could also act as a donor for electrons, which facilitated the enhancement of the carrier density of SnOx/N-GQDs.

The carrier densities of SnO2, SnOx and SnOx/N-GQDs were compared in dark and light fields. According to formulas (2), (3), the carrier densities were estimated to be 4.96 × 1020 cm−3, 1.11 × 1021 cm−3 and 1.59 × 1021 cm−3 in dark, and 6.22 × 1020 cm−3, 1.16 × 1021 cm−3 and 1.76 × 1021 cm−3 in light, respectively. The result showed that the material in light field presented higher carrier density since the ultraviolet illumination was more conducive to reducing the interfacial impedance and improving the electron transport. This result was consistent with the analysis results of LSV, EIS and IT. The introduction of oxygen vacancy was conducive to improving the photoelectric properties of SnO2, showing higher carrier density and migration rate [60].

Fig. 13 displayed the photoelectric mechanism of SnOx/N-GQDs composites. SnO2 and tin powder introduced abundant oxygen vacancies during high temperature nitrogen annealing and the relevant reactions were as follows:

The photoelectric properties of SnOx/N-GQDs were promoted by the introduction of appropriate oxygen vacancies and N-GQDs. The introduction of oxygen vacancies created a defect energy level between the CB and VB of SnO2, and the defect energy level was close to the CB of SnO2. The presence of the defect energy level effectively reduced the band gap of SnO2 and enhanced the utilization of light. The electrons generated from SnO2 VB were firstly trapped by the defect energy level, and then further transported to the nearby SnO2 CB [61,62]. Compared with the direct excitation of electrons from the VB to the CB, this method of electron transport required less energy and generated more electrons, and to a certain extent avoided the recombination of photoelectrons and holes. In view of excessive oxygen vacancies would have a strong binding effect on electrons and hinder further electron transport, the appropriate amount of oxygen vacancies was required to improve the electron transport mode, making SnOx exhibit enhanced photoelectric properties [40]. The addition of N-GQDs formed a heterojunction with SnOx. N-GQDs could be used as both a donor for photogenerated electrons and an intermediate carrier for electron transport, which not only reduced the recombination of electrons and holes, but also accelerated the charge transport and increased the carrier density. Because of the small size and high surface energy of N-GQDs, too much doping could lead to uneven dispersion and agglomeration, which affected the storage and transport of charges in N-GQDs [63,64]. Therefore SnOx/N-GQDs showed enhanced photoelectric properties with the appropriate amount of oxygen vacancies and N-GQDs together.

In conclusion, SnOx/N-GQDs composites were successfully synthesized by thermal annealing and hydrothermal methods. The results showed that N-GQDs were successfully doped into the composites and a large number of oxygen vacancies and defect energy levels were introduced after high temperature annealing. Related electrochemical tests showed that the insertion of oxygen vacancy and doping N-GQDs improve the electron transport mode by regulating the SnO2 properties, hindering the recombination of electron-hole pairs, extending the carrier lifetime (7.44 ms), accelerating the transport of carriers and increasing the carrier density (1.76 × 1021 cm−3 in light). When the Sn: SnO2 was 1: 4 and the N-GQDs content was 1 wt%, the photocurrent density of the SnOx/N-GQDs reached 5.51 × 10−4 A/cm2, which was 25 times higher than that of pure SnO2, exhibiting enhanced photoelectric properties.

Yongqin Wang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Tao Feng: Formal analysis. Lin Li: Funding acquisition. Yun Lei: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Beibei Du: Data curation. Linhui Luo: Visualization. Can Li: Investigation. Yifan Deng: Validation. Zehui Tang: Resources. Jiong Chen: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Key Laboratory for Research and Development of New Lithium-ion Battery Materials (2023-028) and Joint Fund of Bijie City and Guizhou University of Engineering Science (BiKeLianhe [2023]12th).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111357.
